meta.title: Asylum in the UK — How to Apply
meta.description: Complete guide to claiming asylum in the UK in 2025–2026.

hero.title: Claiming Asylum in the UK
hero.sub: If you are fleeing persecution, war, torture or serious harm because of your race, religion, nationality, political opinion or social group — you have a legal right to claim asylum in the UK. This pathway is free, protected by international law, and gives you access to accommodation, food and support while your case is considered.

alert.text: Claiming asylum is a legal right — not a crime. The UK is legally obligated under the 1951 Refugee Convention to consider your claim. You do not need documents to claim asylum. You do not need to pay anyone. If someone charges you to "submit" your claim — they are scamming you.

eligibility: You are outside your home country (or the country you last lived in).
You cannot return because you have a well-founded fear of persecution.
Persecution is based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership of a particular social group (including gender identity and sexual orientation).
You are in the UK, have arrived at a UK border, or intend to arrive.
You have not already been granted refugee status in another safe country.

steps.title: Tell the authorities you want to claim asylum
steps.desc: Tell a UK Border Force officer as soon as you arrive at a port or airport, or call the Home Office on 0300 123 7010 if you are already in the UK. Say the words "I want to claim asylum." You will not be arrested for doing this.

steps.title: Screening interview (Registration)
steps.desc: A short interview to record your basic details, photograph, and fingerprints. You will be asked how you entered the UK and why you left your country. This is not your full asylum interview — just registration.

steps.title: Asylum support & accommodation
steps.desc: While waiting for a decision, the Home Office provides free accommodation and £49.18/week on a payment card if you cannot support yourself. You are legally entitled to this support.

steps.title: Asylum interview (Substantive interview)
steps.desc: A detailed interview with a Home Office caseworker about why you left and why you cannot return. You can bring a legal representative. You can request an interpreter. Take your time — accuracy matters more than speed.

steps.title: Decision
steps.desc: You will receive a written decision — usually within 6 months of your interview. If granted refugee status, you receive 5 years' leave to remain, the right to work, and access to public funds.

steps.title: If refused — appeal
steps.desc: You have the right to appeal a refusal to the First-tier Tribunal (Immigration and Asylum Chamber). Most asylum cases have strong appeal rights. Get legal representation before the appeal deadline (usually 14 days).

after: 5 years' leave to remain in the UK (renewable).
Full right to work in any sector, with any employer.
Access to the NHS, schools, benefits, and social housing.
Right to travel on a UK Refugee Travel Document.
Right to apply for family reunification — bring your spouse and children.
Path to Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR) after 5 years, then British citizenship.

changes.date: 2025 — Border Security, Asylum and Immigration Bill
changes.title: New processing reforms
changes.desc: The 2025 Bill introduced measures to speed up case processing and improve the backlog. The government committed to deciding straightforward cases within 6 months.

changes.title: Some provisions paused — legal challenges ongoing
changes.desc: Parts of the Illegal Migration Act 2023 that would have blocked asylum claims were paused by courts. As of 2026, the standard asylum process remains available.

changes.title: Weekly support payment raised to £49.18
changes.desc: The weekly asylum support allowance was increased from £47.39 to £49.18 per person per week.

changes.title: You can apply to work after 12 months of waiting
changes.desc: If your initial asylum decision has not been made within 12 months through no fault of your own, you can apply for permission to work in any role on the Shortage Occupation List.

warning: Do not pay a smuggler when you can claim asylum legally. Every year, thousands pay €3,000–€15,000 to cross the Channel — only to claim asylum on arrival anyway. Contact Migrant Help free on 0808 801 0503.
